title: Pea and mint soup
subtitle: Frozen peas blended with stock and mint, served hot or cold.
excerpt: Frozen peas blended with vegetable stock, a handful of mint, and a spoonful of crème fraîche. Ready in fifteen minutes. Equally good served hot in winter or chilled in summer.
sourceNotes: Pea and mint soup became a British spring staple through the 1970s and 1980s, drawing on the long British tradition of fresh pea soups documented in cookery books from at least the seventeenth century. The modern version almost always uses frozen peas rather than fresh shelled peas, a swap that works perfectly since frozen peas are processed immediately after picking and retain their colour and sweetness.

content.text: Pea and mint soup is one of the fastest soups you can make. Ten minutes, five ingredients, one pot. The peas need only the briefest cooking: they go from frozen to blended in under five minutes, and keeping that time short is what preserves the bright green colour.
Serve it hot with warm bread, or chill it and serve as a cold soup. Both work. The mint carries differently in each: sharper and more fragrant when hot, quieter and more herbal when cold.
A small or medium saucepan and a stick blender are all you need.
Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a medium saucepan over a low heat. Add the shallots and cook gently for 5 minutes until softened but not coloured.

Tip in {{peas-frozen}} of frozen peas and pour over {{stock-vegetable}} of hot stock. Bring to a boil, then simmer for just 3 minutes. Do not cook longer, the peas should be barely cooked to keep their colour.

Remove from the heat. Add the mint leaves. Blend immediately until smooth with a stick blender. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and {{black-pepper}} of black pepper. Serve straight away with a spoonful of crème fraîche in each bowl.

items.fix: Keep the simmer to 3 minutes and blend immediately. Serve straight away.
items.cause: Peas overcooked or blended too long after sitting off the heat.
items.symptom: Soup is grey-green rather than bright

items.fix: Add more hot stock or water and blend again briefly.
items.cause: Stock ratio was too low for the peas.

items.fix: Start with less mint and adjust. Spearmint is the variety to use; peppermint is too strong for soup.
items.cause: Too many mint leaves, or peppermint rather than garden mint used.
items.symptom: Mint tastes sharp and almost medicinal

items.fix: Pass through a fine sieve for a completely smooth result.
items.cause: Skin on the peas not fully broken down.

content.text: Fresh pea soups appear in British cookery books from the seventeenth century onwards. The frozen-pea version became possible and then ubiquitous once domestic freezers became standard in the 1960s and 1970s. It is a spring and early summer soup: the mint is at its freshest then, the frozen peas are always the same, and the combination reads as a small seasonal ritual. The chilled version, while equally valid, is perhaps the one more likely to appear at a dinner party.
